first_paragraph: **New South Wales politics** takes place in the context of a bicameral parliamentary system. The main parties are the [Liberal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party") and [National](/wiki/New_South_Wales_National_Party "New South Wales National Party") parties of the [Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(New_South_Wales) "Coalition (New South Wales)"), and the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"). Other minor political parties include the [Greens](/wiki/The_Greens_NSW "The Greens NSW"), [Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party](/wiki/Shooters,_Fishers_and_Farmers_Party "Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party"), [One Nation](/wiki/Pauline_Hanson%27s_One_Nation "Pauline Hanson's One Nation"), and the [Libertarian Party](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(Australia) "Libertarian Party (Australia)"), along with several independent politicians.

**New South Wales politics** takes place in the context of a bicameral parliamentary system. The main parties are the [Liberal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party") and [National](/wiki/New_South_Wales_National_Party "New South Wales National Party") parties of the [Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(New_South_Wales) "Coalition (New South Wales)"), and the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"). Other minor political parties include the [Greens](/wiki/The_Greens_NSW "The Greens NSW"), [Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party](/wiki/Shooters,_Fishers_and_Farmers_Party "Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party"), [One Nation](/wiki/Pauline_Hanson%27s_One_Nation "Pauline Hanson's One Nation"), and the [Libertarian Party](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(Australia) "Libertarian Party (Australia)"), along with several independent politicians.

[The executive government (called the NSW Government)](/wiki/NSW_government "NSW government") comprises 11 portfolios, led by a ministerial department and supported by several agencies. There are also a number of independent agencies that fall under a portfolio but remain at arms-length for political reasons, such as the [Independent Commission Against Corruption](/wiki/Independent_Commission_Against_Corruption_(New_South_Wales) "Independent Commission Against Corruption (New South Wales)") and [Electoral Commission](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Electoral_Commission "New South Wales Electoral Commission"). The state [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_New_South_Wales "Executive Council of New South Wales"), consisting of the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales") and senior ministers, exercises the executive authority through the relevant portfolio.

The legislative branch includes the bicameral [state parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales"), which includes the monarchy as represented by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), the [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly"), and [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council"). The judicial branch consists of three general courts ([Local](/wiki/Local_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Local Court of New South Wales"), [District](/wiki/District_Court_of_New_South_Wales "District Court of New South Wales") and [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Supreme Court of New South Wales")), and several specialist courts such as the [Children's Court](/wiki/Children%27s_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Children's Court of New South Wales") or [Coroner's Court](/wiki/Coroner%27s_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Coroner's Court of New South Wales").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

New South Wales received [statehood](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") upon the [federation of Australia](/wiki/Federation_of_Australia "Federation of Australia") in 1901, with the [state's Constitution](/wiki/New_South_Wales_constitution "New South Wales constitution") establishing a parliamentary democracy. Its relationship with the federal government is regulated by the [Australian Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia"). The current government is held by the [state Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"), led by Premier [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns"). Minns succeeded [Dominic Perrottet](/wiki/Dominic_Perrottet "Dominic Perrottet") from the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia_(New_South_Wales_Division) "Liberal Party of Australia (New South Wales Division)") on 28 March 2023 following the [state election](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election").

The New South Wales Legislative Assembly (lower house where government is formed) is sometimes referred to informally as "the bear pit", as a mark of perceived unruly behaviour within the parliamentary chambers, and 'Macquarie Street', a [metonym](/wiki/Metonym "Metonym") of the [street of that name](/wiki/Macquarie_Street,_Sydney "Macquarie Street, Sydney") where [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Sydney "Parliament House, Sydney") is located, in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney")'s CBD.

## State politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: State politics")]

### Parliament of New South Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Parliament of New South Wales")]

Main article: [Government of New South Wales](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales")

The Australian state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") has a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") parliament. The Legislative Assembly (lower house) is composed of 93 members of parliament, each of whom represents a single electorate. The voting system is [preferential](/wiki/Australian_electoral_system "Australian electoral system"). Until the mid-1990s, members of the Assembly served for up to four years, until the [Greiner](/wiki/Nick_Greiner "Nick Greiner") government made terms a fixed length of four years. The Legislative Council (upper house) comprises 42 members, who serve terms of 8 years. The [King](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Australia "Monarchy of Australia") is represented by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), who formally appoints the premier, as nominated by the majority party in the Assembly.

### Office holders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Office holders")]

The formal chief executive of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") is the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales"), who is appointed as [the King](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")'s representative on the advice of the head of the governing party. The current governor is [Margaret Beazley](/wiki/Margaret_Beazley "Margaret Beazley"). The governor holds limited [reserve powers](/wiki/Reserve_powers "Reserve powers"), but with few exceptions is required by convention to act on the advice of the government.

The [Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") is currently [Chris Minns](/wiki/Chris_Minns "Chris Minns") of the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"). The 47th Premier, Minns assumed office on 28 March 2023. The [Deputy Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Deputy_Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Deputy Premier of New South Wales") is [Prue Car](/wiki/Prue_Car "Prue Car").

Officially opposing the [New South Wales government](/wiki/New_South_Wales_government "New South Wales government") is the [opposition](/wiki/Opposition_(New_South_Wales) "Opposition (New South Wales)") [Liberal–National Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(New_South_Wales) "Coalition (New South Wales)")

The government is decided every four years by election. The most recent election [was held in 2023](/wiki/2023_New_South_Wales_state_election "2023 New South Wales state election"), with the next in [2027](/wiki/2027_New_South_Wales_state_election "2027 New South Wales state election").

### Political parties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Political parties")]

See also: [List of political parties in Australia](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_Australia "List of political parties in Australia")

New South Wales is currently governed by the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"). The two main parties are the [Liberal Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Liberal_Party "New South Wales Liberal Party")/[National Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_National_Party "New South Wales National Party") [Coalition](/wiki/Coalition_(New_South_Wales) "Coalition (New South Wales)"), and the [Labor Party](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Labor_Party "New South Wales Labor Party"). Other currently elected parties in New South Wales politics include the [Greens](/wiki/The_Greens_NSW "The Greens NSW"), the [Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party](/wiki/Shooters,_Fishers_and_Farmers_Party "Shooters, Fishers and Farmers Party"), the [Animal Justice Party](/wiki/Animal_Justice_Party "Animal Justice Party"), [One Nation](/wiki/Pauline_Hanson%27s_One_Nation "Pauline Hanson's One Nation"), and the [Libertarian Party](/wiki/Libertarian_Party_(Australia) "Libertarian Party (Australia)"), along with multiple independents.

## Political structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Political structure")]

New South Wales is governed according to the principles of the [Westminster system](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system"), a form of parliamentary government based on the model of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Legislative power formally rests with the King, acting with the advice and consent of the [Legislative Council](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Council "New South Wales Legislative Council") and [Legislative Assembly](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Legislative_Assembly "New South Wales Legislative Assembly")—together known as the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Executive power is exercised by the Executive Council, which consists of the Governor and senior ministers.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Governor, as representative of [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), is the formal repository of power, which is exercised by him or her on the advice of the [Premier of New South Wales](/wiki/Premier_of_New_South_Wales "Premier of New South Wales") and the cabinet.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Premier and ministers are appointed by the Governor, and hold office by virtue of their ability to command the support of a majority of members of the Legislative Assembly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Judicial power is exercised by the [Supreme Court of New South Wales](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Supreme Court of New South Wales") and a system of subordinate courts, but the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") and other federal courts have overriding jurisdiction on matters which fall under the ambit of the Australian Constitution.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2006, the [sesquicentenary](/wiki/Sesquicentenary "Sesquicentenary") of [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government") in New South Wales, the *[Constitution Amendment (Pledge of Loyalty) Act 2006](/wiki/Constitution_Amendment_(Pledge_of_Loyalty)_Act_2006 "Constitution Amendment (Pledge of Loyalty) Act 2006")* No. 6 was enacted to amend the *Constitution Act 1902* to require members of the New South Wales Parliament and its Ministers to take a pledge of loyalty to Australia and to the people of New South Wales instead of swearing allegiance to [the Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), her heirs and successors, and to revise the oaths taken by Executive Councillors.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The Act was assented to by the Queen on 3 April 2006.

On 5 June 2012, the *Constitution Amendment (Restoration of Oaths of Allegiance) Act 2012* No 33 was assented to and made a further amendment to the *Constitution Act 1902*, by restoring the option of taking the oath of allegiance to the Queen, her heirs and successors, in addition to the option of taking the pledge of loyalty.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The change applies to members of Legislative Council, Legislative Assembly and [Executive Council](/wiki/Executive_Council_of_New_South_Wales "Executive Council of New South Wales").

## State party support by region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: State party support by region")]

### Liberal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Liberal")]

The Liberals strongest base has always been on the [North Shore](/wiki/North_Shore_(Sydney) "North Shore (Sydney)") and [Northern Beaches](/wiki/Northern_Beaches "Northern Beaches") as well as the [Hills](/wiki/Hills_District,_New_South_Wales "Hills District, New South Wales") and [Forest](/wiki/Forest_District_(Sydney) "Forest District (Sydney)") districts, creating a 'bloc' on the northern side of Sydney Harbour. The last time the Labor party won an electorate wholly within any of these districts was the electorates of [Manly](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Manly "Electoral district of Manly"), [Wakehurst](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Wakehurst "Electoral district of Wakehurst") and [Willoughby](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Willoughby "Electoral district of Willoughby") in the [1978 'Wranslide' election](/wiki/1978_New_South_Wales_state_election "1978 New South Wales state election"). The electoral districts of [North Shore](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_North_Shore "Electoral district of North Shore") and the single Liberal held electorate of [Vaucluse](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Vaucluse "Electoral district of Vaucluse") in the [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") are the most affluent areas in the state and have never been lost to the Labor party. In recent decades, the Liberals have consistently held the regional electorates of [Albury](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Albury "Electoral district of Albury") and [Goulburn](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Goulburn "Electoral district of Goulburn").

### Nationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Nationals")]

The Nationals (formerly the Country Party) are a party representing country issues and farmers and only generally seek to represent rural and regional electorates. They are ideologically conservative and sit on the centre-right of the political spectrum. Their strongest base within the state has always been the [New England](/wiki/New_England_(New_South_Wales) "New England (New South Wales)"), [Northern Tablelands](/wiki/Northern_Tablelands "Northern Tablelands"), [Northern Rivers](/wiki/Northern_Rivers "Northern Rivers"), [Mid North Coast](/wiki/Mid_North_Coast "Mid North Coast"), [Riverina](/wiki/Riverina "Riverina") and the [Central West](/wiki/Central_West_(New_South_Wales) "Central West (New South Wales)"). The Nationals biggest competitors are the Shooters, Fishers and Farmers and well as local independents from time to time. When there is no incumbent, it is not uncommon for the Liberals to run candidates against the Nationals creating three cornered contests in semi-rural electorates such as [Cessnock](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Cessnock "Electoral district of Cessnock"), [Monaro](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Monaro "Electoral district of Monaro"), [Goulburn](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Goulburn "Electoral district of Goulburn") and [Wagga Wagga](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Wagga_Wagga "Electoral district of Wagga Wagga").

### Labor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Labor")]

Labor was traditionally strongest in the [Inner West](/wiki/Inner_West "Inner West"), [Western Sydney](/wiki/Western_Sydney "Western Sydney") and [South Western Sydney](/wiki/South_Western_Sydney "South Western Sydney"); however, their status has diminished since the late 2000s in the Inner West with the rise of the [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") Greens in the electorates of [Balmain](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Balmain "Electoral district of Balmain") and [Newtown](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Newtown "Electoral district of Newtown"). Labor's significant majorities and continual hold-outs with its highest [two-party preferred](/wiki/Two-party_preferred "Two-party preferred") votes are in the electorates with the highest concentration of [lower socioeconomic](/wiki/Lower_socioeconomic_groups "Lower socioeconomic groups") groups such as [Mount Druitt](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Mount_Druitt "Electoral district of Mount Druitt"), [Blacktown](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Blacktown "Electoral district of Blacktown") and [Canterbury](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Canterbury "Electoral district of Canterbury"). Labor are equally unchallenged in the electorates with known [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave") such as [Lakemba](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Lakemba "Electoral district of Lakemba"), [Cabramatta](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Cabramatta "Electoral district of Cabramatta"), [Bankstown](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Bankstown "Electoral district of Bankstown") and [Fairfield](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Fairfield "Electoral district of Fairfield") respectively.

Outside metropolitan Sydney, Labor have consistently recorded majorities in the regions of the [Hunter](/wiki/Hunter_Region "Hunter Region") and [Central Coast](/wiki/Central_Coast_(New_South_Wales) "Central Coast (New South Wales)") with the Coalition holding only a single electorate in each without interruption, being: [Upper Hunter](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Upper_Hunter "Electoral district of Upper Hunter") and [Terrigal](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Terrigal "Electoral district of Terrigal"). Labor generally performs well in the [Illawarra](/wiki/Illawarra "Illawarra") and in the [Far West](/wiki/Far_West_(New_South_Wales) "Far West (New South Wales)") mining town of [Broken Hill](/wiki/Broken_Hill "Broken Hill").

### Greens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Greens")]

The Greens have solidified support within the Inner West city region of metropolitan Sydney at the expense of Labor. There has been little to no opposition from the Liberals or their predecessors in seats where left-wing candidates have always won by substantial margins such as the current [Balmain](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Balmain "Electoral district of Balmain") and [Newtown](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Newtown "Electoral district of Newtown") and the former related seats such as [Leichhardt](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Leichhardt_(New_South_Wales) "Electoral district of Leichhardt (New South Wales)"), [Phillip](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Phillip "Electoral district of Phillip"), [Elizabeth](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Elizabeth_(New_South_Wales) "Electoral district of Elizabeth (New South Wales)"), [Rozelle](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Rozelle "Electoral district of Rozelle") and [Port Jackson](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Port_Jackson "Electoral district of Port Jackson"). With the loss of these reliable seats, this creates a harder task for Labor to form majority government into the future. The Greens have seen localised success in the Northern Rivers seat of [Ballina](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Ballina "Electoral district of Ballina") which entirely encompasses the [Byron Bay](/wiki/Byron_Shire "Byron Shire") district.

### Marginal seats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Marginal seats")]

For governments to change hands, generally there is a quantity of [marginal electorates](/wiki/Marginal_seat "Marginal seat") that determine the result of the election which sustain the most attention from the major parties. In New South Wales, most of these electorates are located in Western Sydney and surrounds and generally after redistribution by the New South Wales Electoral Commission they remain marginal or ± 5% of the previous margin. In the case of electorates that more than often side with the incoming or continuing government, known as a [bellwether](/wiki/Bellwether_(politics) "Bellwether (politics)"), the electorate of [Monaro](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Monaro "Electoral district of Monaro") holds the record for all but two (1995 and 1999) elections since 1932 in having sided with the government of the day. Other common bellwethers being [Oatley](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Oatley "Electoral district of Oatley") (formerly [Georges River](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Georges_River "Electoral district of Georges River")) and [Ryde](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Ryde "Electoral district of Ryde") (formerly [Gladesville](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Gladesville "Electoral district of Gladesville") and [Fuller](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Fuller "Electoral district of Fuller")). Other electorates that often change hands between the major parties include: [Drummoyne](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Drummoyne "Electoral district of Drummoyne"), [Gosford](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Gosford "Electoral district of Gosford"), [Heathcote](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Heathcote "Electoral district of Heathcote"), [Holsworthy](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Holsworthy "Electoral district of Holsworthy"), [Parramatta](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Parramatta "Electoral district of Parramatta") and [Penrith](/wiki/Electoral_district_of_Penrith "Electoral district of Penrith").

## Federal politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Federal politics")]

New South Wales has 47 seats in the [Australian House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"), the most of any state. As such, it is nearly impossible to win government without a strong base in New South Wales, while a decent showing in New South Wales can usually make up for a poor night elsewhere. Labor has never won an election without winning a majority in New South Wales.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The [1996 federal election](/wiki/1996_Australian_federal_election "1996 Australian federal election") was an example of how critical New South Wales is in federal elections. The election turned into a Coalition rout in large part due to Labor losing 13 of its 33 seats in New South Wales.

### Party support by region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Party support by region")]

### Liberals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Liberals")]

Like at the state level, the federal party draws most of its continuous support from north-west Sydney and the surrounds of the [Hawkesbury River](/wiki/Hawkesbury_River "Hawkesbury River"). However this has eroded in recent elections with the rise of the [Teal independents](/wiki/Teal_independents "Teal independents"). Only the divisons of [Mitchell](/wiki/Division_of_Mitchell "Division of Mitchell") and [Berowra](/wiki/Division_of_Berowra "Division of Berowra") remain with the Liberal Party north of Sydney Harbour. The Party also has strong bases in Southern Sydney and Southern NSW, having continuously held the divisions of [Cook](/wiki/Division_of_Cook "Division of Cook"), [Farrer](/wiki/Division_of_Farrer "Division of Farrer") and [Hume](/wiki/Division_of_Hume "Division of Hume") for several decades. The party lost its traditional harbour-side base of seats including [North Sydney](/wiki/Division_of_North_Sydney "Division of North Sydney"), [Warringah](/wiki/Division_of_Warringah "Division of Warringah"), [Bradfield](/wiki/Division_of_Bradfield "Division of Bradfield"), [Mackellar](/wiki/Division_of_Mackellar "Division of Mackellar") and [Wentworth](/wiki/Division_of_Wentworth "Division of Wentworth") to the teal movement between 2018 and 2025.

### Labor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Labor")]

Unlike with state results, Labor has consistently maintained dominance over the Greens in the Inner West and the Liberals through the bulk of the Western Sydney basin to the Blue Mountains. The only inner-metropolitan Sydney seat that changes between Labor and Liberal is the division of [Reid](/wiki/Division_of_Reid "Division of Reid") (formerly [Lowe](/wiki/Division_of_Lowe "Division of Lowe")) which is currently held by Labor as of 2022. Labor dominate the Hunter, Illawarra and South Coast regions, as of 2022 holding every seat. However, the divisions of [Dobell](/wiki/Division_of_Dobell "Division of Dobell"), [Gilmore](/wiki/Division_of_Gilmore "Division of Gilmore"), [Hunter](/wiki/Division_of_Hunter "Division of Hunter") and [Paterson](/wiki/Division_of_Paterson "Division of Paterson") are considered winnable seats for the Liberals in each region. The Central Coast seat of [Robertson](/wiki/Division_of_Robertson "Division of Robertson") is the longest-continuous [bellwether](/wiki/Bellwether_(politics) "Bellwether (politics)") in the country.

## Notable New South Wales political figures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Politics_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notable New South Wales political figures")]

* [Henry Parkes](/wiki/Henry_Parkes "Henry Parkes"), 6th premier of New South Wales, longest-serving premier, regarded as the Father of Australian Federation
* [Jack Lang](/wiki/Jack_Lang_(Australian_politician) "Jack Lang (Australian politician)"), 23rd premier. Dismissed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales") in 1932.
* Sir [Robert Askin](/wiki/Robert_Askin "Robert Askin"), 32nd premier, notable for his long tenure in office and corrupt behaviour.
* [Nick Greiner](/wiki/Nick_Greiner "Nick Greiner"), 37th premier. Widespread reforms and turbulent premiership.
* [Bob Carr](/wiki/Bob_Carr "Bob Carr"), 39th premier. Longest continual premiership and continued electoral success.
